// ---- SIGNATURE FORMATTING ----
//
//   Same rules as algorithm template:
//   - Align template continuations under first param
//   - Return type + name on one line, opening ( at end
//   - Function params: 4-space indent, 1-2 per line, ~80 cols
//
// ---- GUIDELINES ----
//
//   - Header block: one-line summary, bold metadata (Defined in, Models)
//   - Synopsis: full class template declaration
//   - Quick Usage: compiled snippet from examples/ via include::example$
//   - Template parameters table with Parameter / Description / Default
//   - Member + Non-member functions: one entry per function, separated by '''
//   - Non-member functions grouped: Structure Access, Structure Modification,
//     Property Map Accessors (add more groups if needed, e.g., Serialization)
//   - Associated Types: table with Type / Description
//   - Operation Complexity: tables by selector choice if applicable
//   - Stability/Invalidation: table + WARNING + code example (mutable types)
//   - All sections are optional. Skip if N/A for this class.
//   - No See Also section (the sidebar nav is sufficient)
//
// ============================================================================

[#sec:class-name]
= Class Name

One-line summary.

*Defined in:* `<boost/graph/header.hpp>` +
*Models:* concept1, concept2, concept3

== Synopsis

[source,cpp]
----
template <class ParamA = defaultA,
          class ParamB = defaultB>
class class_name { ... };
----

Description of the class: what it is, what trade-offs the template
parameters control. Keep it short (3-5 lines). Use bullet points for
the key parameter choices if there are several.

== Quick Usage

// Compiled snippet from examples/. Shows the most common configuration:
// create a graph, add edges, traverse. ~15 lines.

[source,cpp]
----
include::example$class_name_usage.cpp[]
----

----
...
----

== Associated Types

// Explain briefly why users need these types (algorithms are templates,
// you need concrete types for variables). Include a short code example.

Algorithms are templates. To declare variables for vertices, edges, or
iterators, extract the concrete types via `graph_traits`:

[source,cpp]
----
using Vertex = graph_traits<Graph>::vertex_descriptor;
----

[cols="1,3",options="header"]
|===
| Type | Description

| `vertex_descriptor` | ...
| `edge_descriptor` | ...
| `vertex_iterator` | ...
|===
